text: The surgeon creates a small window in the mesentery at the proximal and distal resection margins. The mesentery is divided using a tissue sealing device close to the intestinal wall, preserving vascularization and innervation. Wet towels cover the exposed intestines to prevent fluid and heat loss.

The surgeon marks the resection points and the anti-mesenteric wall by drawing a line on the intestinal wall. The bowel is then divided transversely with the GIA stapler placed perpendicular to the mesentery, positioning the mesentery in the middle of the staple lines. On the colon side, the anti-mesenteric wall is situated between the two tinea coli.

The surgeon imbricates and reinforces the corners of the two staple lines using absorbable Lambert sutures. The corresponding corner sutures are tied together to approximate the two intestinal segments for anastomosis. Interrupted absorbable sutures are used to sew the staple lines together, creating a supporting column for stable anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an antimesenteric longitudinal enterotomy and colostomy on each stump, creating a transverse lumen of seven centimeters on the small bowel and closer to eight centimeters on the colon. The anastomosis is completed by closing the longitudinal opening transversely in a single-layer gambi manner using trio or foro absorbable running sutures, starting with two sutures in the middle on the posterior or mesenteric aspect and joining them on the anterior or anti-mesenteric aspect.

The surgeon covers the anastomosis with omentum to prevent adhesions at the anastomotic site, following the division of the mesentery close to the intestinal wall, which results in a minimal mesenteric defect.
